preamble: The shift from the postalveolar fricative [ʃ] to the velar fricative [x] is a classic example of consonant retraction. This sound change was a defining feature of the 'Spanish Sibilant Shift' in the 16th and 17th centuries, where earlier hushing sounds moved further back in the vocal tract to increase their phonetic distance from other sibilants.

examples.note: The Medieval hushing sound (written as x) shifted systematically to the velar position.
While the spelling x is preserved, the sound has retracted to the velar fricative.
Ongoing retraction of loanwords or dialectal sibilants in some South American varieties.

sources: Penny: A History of the Spanish Language
